title: Misdiagnosis surfaced and corrected mid-conversation: "extend the wire for browsers" was the wrong move; the MCP wrapper is the answer
content: Operator asked the strategic question "what would it take to make the mock theatre a real-time demo showcase?" Initial response (this session, ~16 min before correction) laid out an option matrix that recommended Phase 1: extend PROTOCOL.md §4.1 to accept Sec-WebSocket-Protocol-as-bearer credential alongside Authorization header, then Phase 2: rewire the homepage theatre's emit()/onToken() functions against the new browser-friendly /connect path. Operator's follow-up question — "couldn't this be solved once we make the MCP wrapper?" — was sharper than the original analysis and immediately exposed the misdiagnosis. The wire-extension proposal violated ams://canon/decisions/D0006-dream-house-wire-edge-wrappers ("the wire does not bend to runtimes; per-session edge wrappers absorb the impedance mismatch") despite the prior response having cited D0006 by name. The browser is a runtime. The MCP wrapper is the canonical edge wrapper for any MCP-speaking runtime. fetch() lets browsers be MCP clients with arbitrary headers (unlike new WebSocket()). Streamable HTTP transport's SSE leg covers server-pushed token notifications. Same-origin homepage → /mcp = no CORS work. The path through the MCP wrapper costs zero additional wrapper development beyond what SPEC §3.2's demo gate already mandates for Day 3, and it satisfies wrapper-stays-cheap by giving the MCP wrapper a 5th class of consumer (Claude Code, Claude Desktop, Cursor, claude.ai, browsers) instead of needing a one-consumer browser-only wrapper.

title: D0012: Browser is an MCP runtime; live web demos use the wrapper, not a wire extension
content: Encoded as canon/decisions/D0012-browser-is-an-mcp-runtime.md (tier 2, semi-stable, two-way reversible). The decision: the wire (PROTOCOL.md) does NOT grow a browser-friendly auth shape; no Sec-WebSocket-Protocol-as-bearer, no cookie auth on /connect, no query-string credential. Browsers reach AMS by issuing JSON-RPC tools/call requests against /mcp over HTTP with Authorization on the fetch(), consuming server-pushed events via the SSE leg of Streamable HTTP transport. The homepage theatre's eventual live-demo path is a small JavaScript IIFE in worker/src/homepage.ts that speaks MCP same-origin against /mcp — no new endpoints, no wire change, no wrapper additions beyond what SPEC §3.2's demo gate already demands. Visitor-minted bearers (programmatic re-use of door (i)'s flow) are non-negotiable; shared embedded credentials in client-side JS are harvestable in seconds. Same-origin co-deployment of homepage + /mcp on ams.klappy.dev / ams.truthkit.ai (per D0011) keeps CORS trivial. Rate limiting belongs at the wrapper layer where it protects every MCP consumer, not as a homepage afterthought.

title: D0012 is downstream of D0006 — accepting wire-extensions for runtime convenience would unravel the foundation play
content: D0006 ("Dream-House Wire, Edge-Wrapped Reality") is a one-way door at the architectural level. Once the wrapper boundary exists, runtimes are expected to plug in via wrappers, not via wire revisions. Extending the wire to add browser-friendly auth would have been the first wire-bending-to-runtime accommodation; the failure mode is that every subsequent runtime asks for the same accommodation and the wire gradually absorbs every runtime's quirks. D0012 makes the rejection explicit for the browser case so the next reader doesn't have to re-derive it under time pressure. The decision does NOT preclude later adding browser-friendly auth to the wire if the MCP wrapper turns out to be unsuitable for a browser-native real-time use case (the reversibility is two-way) — but the default is "wrapper first; direct wire path only on demonstrated need."

title: Citing canon and following canon are different actions — the prior turn cited D0006 and then proposed exactly the move D0006 forbids
content: The misdiagnosis is instructive. The prior turn's response named D0006 and the wrapper-stays-cheap constraint, then proceeded to recommend Sec-WebSocket-Protocol-as-bearer as Option 1 — the move D0006 explicitly rejects ("the wire does not bend to runtimes"). The reasoning was internally consistent within a wrong frame: I asked "what's the smallest wire change that lets browsers connect?" instead of "is a wire change appropriate at all?" The correct first question, per D0006, is the latter. The operator's follow-up forced the correct frame; without it, this PR could have shipped a wire-spec extension that accumulated technical and conceptual debt against the foundation play. Generalizable rule for future planning conversations: when a recommendation involves changing the wire, check D0006 against it before listing the option. If D0006 says "this is what wrappers are for," the wire change is not on the option list at all.

title: Dumb question was the sharpest move in the conversation
content: The operator framed the corrective question as "Dumb question, couldn't this be solved once we make the MCP wrapper?" The "dumb question" framing is worth noticing: it was the exact opposite of dumb — it was the question that surfaced the architectural answer the prior analysis missed. Recording so the pattern is legible: when an operator prefaces a question with "dumb question," the right response is to take it dead-seriously, because the framing usually means the operator is checking whether the over-engineered analysis missed something obvious. In this case it had.

title: Canon artifact: canon/decisions/D0012-browser-is-an-mcp-runtime.md (tier 2, semi-stable, status active)
content: Frontmatter: uri ams://canon/decisions/D0012-browser-is-an-mcp-runtime, derives_from D0006 + mcp-wrapper-conformance + wrapper-stays-cheap + POC-INFRA.md §3 §4 + this journal entry, complements D0006 + mcp-wrapper-conformance + wrapper-stays-cheap + permanent-non-goals, governs how browser-based AMS clients reach the wire. Sections: pull-quote summary, The Decision, What This Means in Practice (5 bullets), Why the Wrapper Not a Wire Extension (4 reasons in descending weight), Why Not Wait for a Different Adapter (cookie/relay/WebTransport/fetch-streaming all rejected with one-line rationale each), What Falls Out For Free (3 bullets), What This Forecloses (3 bullets), Reversibility (two-way, asymmetric — adding direct wire path later is cheap; removing it once added is expensive, hence "wrapper first" default), See Also (6 cross-references). One single new file in canon/decisions/. No SPEC, PROTOCOL, ARCHITECTURE, POC-INFRA, POC-PLAN, or worker source changes. No new endpoints. The decision constrains FUTURE work (the homepage live-demo path post-MCP-wrapper) — it does not change any current code.

title: D0012 sits on its own branch + PR; downstream homepage live-demo work is gated on MCP wrapper landing
content: Branch claude/canon-d0012-browser-as-mcp-runtime off main at 5f01e0e (Day 2 merge). Single commit. New file: canon/decisions/D0012-browser-is-an-mcp-runtime.md. New file: this journal entry. Deliberately separate from PR #15 (the homepage post-Day-2 reality + AMS acronym work) because (a) D0012 is a forward-looking decision doc, not a code change, (b) it can land independently of PR #15, (c) keeping decision docs on their own PR makes the canon graph easier to read for future sessions. Per AGENTS.md mode discipline, validation of the decision's clarity + cross-references + alignment with D0006 and wrapper-stays-cheap is a fresh-session job. The downstream consequence — actually wiring the homepage theatre to the MCP wrapper for live demo — is gated on (1) the MCP wrapper shipping per Day 3 of POC-PLAN.md, (2) SPEC §3.1 items 4 + 5 passing (the MCP-mediated round-trip tests), (3) a post-PoC explicit prioritization of the homepage live-demo work which is NOT a PoC requirement (the PoC ships when SPEC §3.2's two-real-Claude-Codes demo passes, not when the homepage SIM goes live). Until those three preconditions are met, the homepage SIM badge text "In-browser SIM (browser WS auth limit)" remains the honest characterization, and D0012 is the canonical answer to anyone who proposes a wire extension to remove it.
